news_summary: Advancements in technology continue to transform everyday life through innovations like space-derived tools, breakthrough silicon compounds, ultra-efficient optical devices, and new possibilities in quantum cloning.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès technologiques continuent de transformer notre quotidien grâce à des innovations telles que les outils dérivés de l'espace, les composés de silicium révolutionnaires, les dispositifs optiques ultra-efficaces et les nouvelles possibilités offertes par le clonage quantique.
news_summary_es: Los avances tecnológicos continúan transformando la vida cotidiana a través de innovaciones como herramientas derivadas del espacio, compuestos de silicio revolucionarios, dispositivos ópticos ultraeficientes y nuevas posibilidades en la clonación cuántica.

articles.title: Technology Originally Developed for Space Missions Now Integral to Everyday Life
articles.summary: Groundbreaking “camera-on-a-chip” technology that was originally developed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) for use in space missions is currently employed in billions of devices like cell phones that are used daily by people worldwide.
articles.content:
Groundbreaking “camera-on-a-chip” technology that was originally developed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) for use in space missions is currently employed in billions of devices like cell phones that are used daily by people worldwide.

Eric Fossum (in the center of the front row) and the team that invented the CMOS image sensor on site at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory. Courtesy NASA/JPL-Caltech

In the 1980s, sensors used to produce high-quality images for space science (including the amazing images from NASA's Hubble Space Telescope) and other applications employed charge coupled device (CCD) technology. Dr. Eric Fossum was originally hired at JPL in 1990 to advance CCD technology for use in interplanetary space missions, but he ended up advancing another technology called complementary metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) technology for that purpose and much more. While at JPL, Fossum took advantage of a technique commonly used for CCDs and applied it to CMOS sensors to develop the first CMOS active pixel image sensor. This development began a chain of events that led to the present use of CMOS technology not only in space science missions, but also in billions of cameras in smartphones, webcams, automobiles, and medical devices used worldwide.

A new technology emerges…

In 1990, CCDs were the primary technology used to generate high-quality images. CCD sensors consist of arrays of pixels that convert light into electric charges. The charge from each pixel is transferred step-by-step to an output amplifier at the corner of the sensor and converted to a voltage that represents the brightness of the light received at the corresponding pixel. The data from all the pixels is then aggregated to generate an image. While CCD cameras can produce very high-quality images that are suitable for scientific use, they require a lot of power and an efficient charge transfer process to be effective.

CMOS sensors, on the other hand, have signal amplifiers within each pixel and signals can be read directly from each pixel instead of being transferred long distances to an amplifier for conversion. CMOS sensors therefore require less voltage to operate than CCDs and issues with the charge transfer process such as radiation susceptibility are greatly reduced. Although CMOS sensors existed in the 1990s, they produced too much noise to produce high-quality images required for science applications.

To reduce the signal noise typical of CMOS sensors at that time, Fossum applied a technique that was often used in CCD devices. This technique—called “intra-pixel charge transfer with correlated double sampling”—enables a double measurement of a pixel’s voltage without and with the light-generated charge. Subtracting the values of these two samples enables noise to be suppressed, improving the signal-to-noise ratio.

The next steps

Soon several companies signed Technology Cooperation Agreements with JPL and partnered with Fossum and his colleagues to develop the promising new technology. In 1995, Fossum and co-worker Dr. Sabrina Kemeny licensed the technology from CalTech and founded a company called Photobit to develop CMOS sensors. In 1996, Fossum left JPL to work at Photobit full time. The Photobit, team further refined the CMOS technology to get it closer to CCD capabilities, reduce power requirements, and make manufacturing cheaper.

Shortly thereafter, CMOS cameras started to be used in webcams, “pill cams” (small, swallowable devices that incorporate a tiny camera to take thousands of high-resolution images of the digestive tract), and other applications. In 2001 Photobit was acquired by Micron Technology, a larger company that devoted even more resources to development of CMOS technology. With the subsequent explosion of the cell phone industry, by 2013 more than a billion CMOS sensors were manufactured each year, and today that number has grown to about seven billion per year.

Where are these sensors now?

The CMOS technology Dr. Fossum originally developed has not only enabled space science, it has been infused into devices we depend on every day, dramatically and positively transforming many aspects of our lives. Virtually all digital still and video cameras, including those on cell phones, employ them. In addition, CMOS technology is used in automotive electronics, webcams, sports cameras, industrial equipment, security cameras including doorbells, and cinematography cameras, and for medical and dental imaging, among many other applications.

A frame from a video made from images taken by the Wide-Field Imager for Solar Probe (WISPR) instrument (which employs CMOS technology) onboard NASA's Parker Solar Probe. This image was captured during the mission’s record-breaking flyby of the Sun on Dec. 25, 2024, and shows the solar wind racing out from the Sun’s outer atmosphere, the corona. Credit: NASA/Johns Hopkins APL/Naval Research Lab

In addition to dominating the commercial and consumer market, CMOS imagers have been used as engineering cameras to enable the entry, descent, and landing of NASA's Perseverance Mars rover, in the camera onboard the OCO-3 (Orbiting Carbon Observatory-3) mission that monitors the distribution of carbon dioxide on Earth, and as scientific imagers on NASA's Parker Solar Probe mission that is revolutionizing our understanding of the Sun. CMOS imagers are on their way to Jupiter’s moon, Europa, on the agency's Europa Clipper mission, and a delta-doped ultraviolet version with tailored response is under development for use on the upcoming UVEX (UltraViolet EXplorer) mission that will provide insight into how galaxies and stars evolve.

CMOS imagers are routinely used in monitoring the launch and deployment of CubeSats and SmallSats. They were recently used to monitor the deployment of Pandora, a small satellite that will characterize exoplanet atmospheres and their host stars; BLACKCAT (the Black Hole Coded Aperture Telescope), a small X-ray telescope; and the SPARCS (Star-Planet Activity Research CubeSat) mission designed to monitor and characterize the stellar flares of low-mass stars in ultraviolet to provide context for the habitability of exoplanets in their system. NASA is also developing descendants of this technology for use in missions that will search for life beyond Earth like its Habitable Worlds Observatory.

In recognition of the impact this CMOS technology has had, the National Academy of Engineering (NAE) has named Dr. Fossum the recipient of the 2026 Charles Stark Draper Prize for Engineering “for innovation, development, and commercialization of the complementary metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) active pixel image sensor ‘camera-on-a-chip.’” The NAE bestows this award biennially to honor an engineer “whose accomplishment has significantly impacted society by improving the quality of life, providing the ability to live freely and comfortably, and/or permitting the access to information.”

Sponsoring Organizations: The original efforts at JPL to develop this CMOS technology were funded by JPL and NASA.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough originally developed for space missions that now benefits billions worldwide through everyday devices like smartphones and medical imaging. It highlights broad, tangible positive impacts on society and details the development and applications of CMOS sensor technology, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s camera-on-a-chip technology now powers billions of everyday devices
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the 1980s, space missions used special cameras called CCD sensors to capture high-quality images. These cameras were important for science but used a lot of power. NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) wanted to improve this technology for space exploration.

**What happened** – In the 1990s, Dr. Eric Fossum at JPL developed a new type of camera sensor called CMOS active pixel sensor. This technology used less power and worked better in space conditions. Fossum and his team improved CMOS sensors to reduce noise and make them clearer. Over time, this technology was licensed to companies and became widely used in devices like smartphones, webcams, medical tools, and cars.

**Impact** – Today, billions of devices around the world use CMOS sensors every day. This technology helps take the pictures on your phone, helps doctors see inside the body, and even supports NASA missions exploring the Sun and Mars. It has changed many parts of daily life and space science by making cameras smaller, cheaper, and more energy-efficient.

**What's next step** – NASA continues to develop new versions of CMOS sensors for future space missions, including exploring Jupiter’s moon Europa and studying distant galaxies. The technology is also being improved to help find signs of life beyond Earth.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A camera technology first developed for space now helps billions of everyday devices capture images, improving both science and daily life worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: La technologie de caméra sur puce de la NASA alimente désormais des milliards d'appareils quotidiens
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les années 1980, les missions spatiales utilisaient des caméras spéciales appelées capteurs CCD pour capturer des images de haute qualité. Ces caméras étaient importantes pour la science mais consommaient beaucoup d'énergie. Le Laboratoire de propulsion de la NASA (JPL) voulait améliorer cette technologie pour l'exploration spatiale.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Dans les années 1990, le Dr Eric Fossum au JPL a développé un nouveau type de capteur d'image appelé capteur actif à pixels CMOS. Cette technologie utilisait moins d'énergie et fonctionnait mieux dans les conditions spatiales. Fossum et son équipe ont amélioré les capteurs CMOS pour réduire le bruit et rendre les images plus nettes. Avec le temps, cette technologie a été licenciée à des entreprises et est devenue largement utilisée dans des appareils comme les smartphones, les webcams, les outils médicaux et les voitures.

**Impact** – Aujourd'hui, des milliards d'appareils dans le monde utilisent des capteurs CMOS chaque jour. Cette technologie aide à prendre les photos sur votre téléphone, aide les médecins à voir à l'intérieur du corps, et soutient même les missions de la NASA explorant le Soleil et Mars. Elle a transformé de nombreux aspects de la vie quotidienne et de la science spatiale en rendant les caméras plus petites, moins chères et plus économes en énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA continue de développer de nouvelles versions des capteurs CMOS pour les futures missions spatiales, notamment l'exploration de la lune Europe de Jupiter et l'étude des galaxies lointaines. La technologie est également améliorée pour aider à rechercher des signes de vie au-delà de la Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une technologie de caméra développée pour l'espace aide désormais des milliards d'appareils quotidiens à capturer des images, améliorant la science et la vie quotidienne dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: La tecnología de cámara en chip de la NASA impulsa ahora miles de millones de dispositivos cotidianos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la década de 1980, las misiones espaciales usaban cámaras especiales llamadas sensores CCD para capturar imágenes de alta calidad. Estas cámaras eran importantes para la ciencia, pero consumían mucha energía. El Laboratorio de Propulsión a Chorro de la NASA (JPL) quería mejorar esta tecnología para la exploración espacial.

**Qué pasó** – En la década de 1990, el Dr. Eric Fossum en JPL desarrolló un nuevo tipo de sensor de cámara llamado sensor activo de píxeles CMOS. Esta tecnología usaba menos energía y funcionaba mejor en condiciones espaciales. Fossum y su equipo mejoraron los sensores CMOS para reducir el ruido y hacer las imágenes más claras. Con el tiempo, esta tecnología fue licenciada a empresas y se usa ampliamente en dispositivos como teléfonos inteligentes, cámaras web, herramientas médicas y automóviles.

**Impacto** – Hoy en día, miles de millones de dispositivos en todo el mundo usan sensores CMOS a diario. Esta tecnología ayuda a tomar las fotos en tu teléfono, ayuda a los médicos a ver dentro del cuerpo y también apoya las misiones de la NASA que exploran el Sol y Marte. Ha cambiado muchas partes de la vida diaria y la ciencia espacial al hacer las cámaras más pequeñas, económicas y con menor consumo de energía.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA sigue desarrollando nuevas versiones de sensores CMOS para futuras misiones espaciales, incluyendo la exploración de la luna Europa de Júpiter y el estudio de galaxias lejanas. La tecnología también se mejora para ayudar a buscar señales de vida fuera de la Tierra.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una tecnología de cámara desarrollada para el espacio ahora ayuda a miles de millones de dispositivos cotidianos a capturar imágenes, mejorando la ciencia y la vida diaria en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A glowing, intricately detailed microchip shaped like a camera lens floats above an open book of space exploration, with softly glowing orbits and abstract representations of planets and satellites surrounding it, all rendered in warm earthy tones and gentle light, symbolizing the fusion of advanced space technology with everyday life.

articles.title: 50 year quest ends with creation of silicon aromatic once thought impossible
articles.summary: After nearly 50 years of failed attempts and scientific speculation, chemists at Saarland University have achieved what many thought might be impossible: creating a long-sought silicon-based aromatic molecule. By replacing carbon atoms in a famously stable ring-shaped compound with silicon, the team synthesized pentasilacyclopentadienide — a breakthrough published in Science.
articles.content:
Major scientific advances often require patience, and this discovery is a prime example. After nearly 50 years of theory and repeated failed attempts by research groups around the world, David Scheschkewitz, Professor of General and Inorganic Chemistry at Saarland University, and his doctoral student Ankur -- collaborating with Bernd Morgenstern from Saarland University's X-Ray Diffraction Service Centre -- have achieved a long sought breakthrough. Their findings have been published in the prestigious journal Science.

So what exactly did the team accomplish? They successfully synthesized pentasilacyclopentadienide, a compound that chemists have tried to create for decades. While the name may sound obscure, the achievement is significant. The researchers replaced the carbon atoms in an aromatic compound -- a class of exceptionally stable molecules in organic chemistry -- with silicon atoms.

Aromatic molecules are essential in modern industry, particularly in plastics manufacturing. "In polyethylene and polypropylene production, for example, aromatic compounds help make the catalysts that control these industrial chemical processes more durable and more effective," explains David Scheschkewitz. Silicon differs fundamentally from carbon because it is more metallic and does not hold onto its electrons as tightly. Substituting silicon for carbon in pentasilacyclopentadienide could therefore lead to entirely new types of compounds and catalysts with distinct properties. That shift opens possibilities for innovative materials and industrial processes.

Why Aromatic Stability Is So Special

The challenge of creating this molecule lies in the unusual stability of aromatic systems. Cyclopentadienide -- the carbon-containing model for the silicon analogue pentasilacyclopentadienide -- is an aromatic hydrocarbon made up of five carbon atoms arranged in a flat ('planar') ring structure -- a shape that contributes to its remarkable stability. (Historical side note: Aromatics were given this name because the first such compounds to be discovered in the second half of the 19th century were found to have particularly distinctive and often pleasant aromas.)

"To be classified as aromatic, a compound needs to have a particular number of shared electrons that are evenly distributed around the planar ring structure, and this number is expressed by Hückel's rule -- a simple mathematical expression named after the German physicist Erich Hückel," explains David Scheschkewitz. Because these electrons are spread evenly around the ring rather than tied to individual atoms, the molecule gains extra stability.

Decades of Failed Attempts Finally Succeed

For many years, chemists knew of only one silicon based aromatic compound. In 1981, researchers created the silicon analogue of cyclopropenium -- an aromatic molecule in which a three membered carbon ring was replaced by a three membered silicon ring. Beyond that, efforts to produce larger silicon based aromatic systems repeatedly failed.

That has now changed. Ankur, Bernd Morgenstern and David Scheschkewitz have synthesized a five atom silicon ring that displays the defining characteristics of aromaticity. Almost simultaneously, Takeaki Iwamoto's group at Tohoku University in Sendai, Japan, independently produced the same compound. The two teams agreed to publish their results side by side in the same issue of Science.

Opening the Door to New Materials and Catalysts

This breakthrough lays the foundation for developing new materials and chemical processes with potential industrial applications. After decades of pursuit, researchers have taken the crucial first step toward expanding the possibilities of silicon based chemistry.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in chemistry: the successful synthesis of a silicon-based aromatic molecule after nearly 50 years of attempts. This achievement has broad implications for industrial chemistry and materials science, potentially leading to new catalysts and materials with distinct properties. The discovery is well-detailed, focuses on a single major advancement, and represents a meaningful step forward with tangible benefits for industry and science.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a new silicon-based aromatic molecule after 50 years of efforts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For almost 50 years, chemists have tried to make a special type of molecule where carbon atoms in a stable ring are replaced by silicon atoms. This idea was hard to achieve because silicon behaves differently from carbon.

**What happened** – A team at Saarland University in Germany, led by Professor David Scheschkewitz and his student Ankur, succeeded in making this molecule called pentasilacyclopentadienide. They replaced the carbon atoms in a well-known stable ring structure with silicon atoms. Their work was published in the scientific journal Science. At the same time, another group in Japan made the same discovery.

**Impact** – This molecule is important because aromatic compounds, which have a special stable ring structure, are used in making plastics and other materials. Replacing carbon with silicon could lead to new materials and better catalysts, which help speed up chemical reactions in industry. This discovery opens new possibilities for creating materials with unique properties.

**What's next step** – Scientists will now explore how this new silicon-based molecule can be used to develop new materials and improve industrial processes. This first success might lead to more discoveries in silicon chemistry and new practical applications.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After decades of trying, scientists have created a new silicon-based aromatic molecule that could change how materials and catalysts are made.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une nouvelle molécule aromatique à base de silicium après 50 ans d’efforts
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant près de 50 ans, les chimistes ont tenté de fabriquer un type spécial de molécule où les atomes de carbone d’un anneau stable sont remplacés par des atomes de silicium. Cette idée était difficile à réaliser car le silicium se comporte différemment du carbone.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Université de la Sarre en Allemagne, dirigée par le professeur David Scheschkewitz et son étudiant Ankur, a réussi à fabriquer cette molécule appelée pentasilacyclopentadiénide. Ils ont remplacé les atomes de carbone dans une structure d’anneau stable par des atomes de silicium. Leur travail a été publié dans la revue scientifique Science. En même temps, un autre groupe au Japon a fait la même découverte.

**Impact** – Cette molécule est importante car les composés aromatiques, qui ont une structure d’anneau particulièrement stable, sont utilisés dans la fabrication de plastiques et d’autres matériaux. Remplacer le carbone par du silicium pourrait conduire à de nouveaux matériaux et à de meilleurs catalyseurs, qui accélèrent les réactions chimiques dans l’industrie. Cette découverte ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour créer des matériaux aux propriétés uniques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont maintenant explorer comment cette nouvelle molécule à base de silicium peut être utilisée pour développer de nouveaux matériaux et améliorer les procédés industriels. Ce premier succès pourrait mener à d’autres découvertes en chimie du silicium et à des applications pratiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Après des décennies d’efforts, des scientifiques ont créé une nouvelle molécule aromatique à base de silicium qui pourrait changer la fabrication des matériaux et des catalyseurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una nueva molécula aromática de silicio tras 50 años de intentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante casi 50 años, los químicos han intentado hacer un tipo especial de molécula donde los átomos de carbono en un anillo estable son reemplazados por átomos de silicio. Esta idea fue difícil de lograr porque el silicio se comporta de manera diferente al carbono.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de la Universidad de Saarland en Alemania, dirigido por el profesor David Scheschkewitz y su estudiante Ankur, logró crear esta molécula llamada pentasilaciclopentadienilo. Reemplazaron los átomos de carbono en una estructura de anillo conocida y estable por átomos de silicio. Su trabajo fue publicado en la revista científica Science. Al mismo tiempo, otro grupo en Japón hizo el mismo descubrimiento.

**Impacto** – Esta molécula es importante porque los compuestos aromáticos, que tienen una estructura de anillo especialmente estable, se usan en la fabricación de plásticos y otros materiales. Reemplazar el carbono por silicio podría llevar a nuevos materiales y mejores catalizadores, que ayudan a acelerar las reacciones químicas en la industria. Este descubrimiento abre nuevas posibilidades para crear materiales con propiedades únicas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos ahora explorarán cómo esta nueva molécula basada en silicio puede usarse para desarrollar nuevos materiales y mejorar procesos industriales. Este primer éxito podría conducir a más descubrimientos en la química del silicio y a aplicaciones prácticas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Tras décadas de intentos, científicos han creado una nueva molécula aromática de silicio que podría cambiar la fabricación de materiales y catalizadores.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, five-membered silicon atom ring gently floating above an open ancient chemistry tome, surrounded by softly illuminated laboratory glassware and delicate crystalline structures, symbolizing decades of patient scientific pursuit and breakthrough in silicon-based aromatic chemistry.

articles.title: Scientists create ultra-low loss optical device that traps light on a chip
articles.summary: CU Boulder researchers have designed microscopic “racetracks” that trap and amplify light with exceptional efficiency. By using smooth curves inspired by highway engineering, they reduced energy loss and kept light circulating longer inside the device. Fabricated with sub-nanometer precision, the resonators rank among the top performers made from chalcogenide glass. The technology could lead to compact sensors, microlasers, and advanced quantum systems.
articles.content:
Researchers at CU Boulder have developed highly efficient optical microresonators that could support a new generation of powerful sensor technologies.

A microresonator is a microscopic structure designed to confine light in a small space. As light circulates inside, its intensity increases. When that intensity reaches a sufficient level, scientists can carry out specialized optical processes that enable sensing and other advanced functions.

"Our work is about using less optical power with these resonators for future uses," said Bright Lu, a fourth year doctoral student in electrical and computer engineering and a lead author on the study. "One day these microresonators can be adapted for a wide range of sensors from navigation to identifying chemicals."

The research was published in Applied Physics Letters.

Racetrack Resonator Design Reduces Light Loss

To achieve stronger performance, the team focused on "racetrack" resonators, which are named for their elongated loop shape that resembles a running track.

They incorporated "Euler curves" -- a type of smooth curve also found in road and railway design. Just as vehicles cannot navigate sudden right angle turns at speed, light does not travel efficiently through sharp bends.

"These racetrack curves minimize bending loss," said Won Park, Sheppard Professor of Electrical Engineering and a co advisor on the project. "Our design choice was a key innovation of this project."

By steering light through gradual, carefully engineered curves, the researchers significantly limited the amount of light that escaped. This allowed photons to circulate longer within the resonator and interact more intensely.

Lu explained that excessive light loss prevents the device from reaching the high intensities required for optimal operation.

Precision Nanofabrication at COSINC

The microresonators were fabricated at the Colorado Shared Instrumentation in Nanofabrication and Characterization (COSINC) clean room using a new electron beam lithography system.

Such facilities maintain tightly controlled conditions that are essential for producing reliable devices at extremely small scales. Many optical and photonic components are smaller than the width of a sheet of paper, so even tiny dust particles or minor surface imperfections can interfere with how light travels through them.

"Traditional lithography uses photons and is fundamentally limited by the wavelength of light," Lu said. "However, electron beam lithography has no such constraint. With electrons, we can realize our structures with sub-nanometer resolution, which is critical for our microresonators."

Lu described the fabrication process as one of the most rewarding parts of the project.

"Clean rooms are just cool. You're working with these massive, precise machines, and then you get to see images of structures you made only microns wide. Turning a thin film of glass into a working optical circuit is really satisfying."

Chalcogenide Glass Enables Ultra Low Loss Performance

A major milestone for the team was successfully building the devices using chalcogenides, a family of specialized semiconductor glasses.

"These chalcogenides are excellent materials for photonics because of their high transparency and nonlinearity," Park said. "Our work represents one of the best performing devices using chalcogenides, if not the best."

Chalcogenides allow intense light to pass through with minimal loss, which is essential for high performance microresonators. At the same time, they are challenging materials to process, requiring careful balance during fabrication.

"Chalcogenides are difficult, but rewarding materials to operate for photonic nonlinear devices," said Professor Juilet Gopinath, who has collaborated with Park on this project for more than 10 years. "Our results showed that minimizing the bend loss enables ultra-low loss devices comparable to state-of-the-art in other materials platforms."

Laser Testing and Resonance Measurements

After fabrication, the devices were evaluated under the leadership of James Erikson, a physics PhD student who specializes in laser based measurements. He precisely aligned lasers with microscopic waveguides to send light into and out of the resonators while monitoring its behavior inside.

The team searched for "dips" in the transmitted light signal that indicate resonance, which occurs when photons become trapped and circulate within the structure. By studying the shape of those dips, they were able to determine properties such as absorption and thermal effects.

"The most obvious indicator of device quality is the shape of the resonances and we want them to be deep and narrow, like a needle piercing through the signal background," Erikson said. "We've been chasing this kind of resonator for a long time, and when we saw the sharp resonances on this new device we knew right away that we'd finally cracked the code."

Erikson noted that understanding how much light is absorbed compared to how much is transmitted is critical for device performance. Increasing laser power can introduce heating, which in turn can alter material properties or even damage the device.

"The way most materials interact with light also changes depending on the temperature of the material," said Erikson. "So as a device heats up its properties can change and cause it to work differently."

Toward Microlasers and Quantum Photonics

Looking ahead, these microresonators could be used to create compact microlasers, highly sensitive chemical and biological sensors, and tools for quantum metrology and networking.

"Many photonic components from lasers, modulators and detectors are being developed and microresonators like ours will help tie all of those pieces together," Lu said. "Eventually, the goal is to build something you could hand to a manufacturer and create hundreds of thousands of them."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological breakthrough in creating ultra-low loss optical microresonators with broad potential applications in sensors, microlasers, and quantum systems. The innovation has meaningful real-world impact by enabling more efficient photonic devices that could benefit many industries and society at large. The article provides detailed context about the design, fabrication, materials, and testing, showing substantive progress in the field.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop tiny optical racetracks that trap light with minimal loss
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Researchers at the University of Colorado Boulder have been working on tiny devices called microresonators. These devices trap and hold light in a small space, allowing it to build up strength for uses like sensing chemicals or improving lasers.

**What happened** – The team created new microresonators shaped like racetracks, using smooth curves inspired by highway design to prevent light from escaping. They made these devices from a special glass called chalcogenide using extremely precise machines in a clean lab. Tests showed the devices hold light better than most others made from similar materials.

**Impact** – This design reduces energy loss and lets light circulate longer inside the device, which is important for making sensors, tiny lasers, and quantum technology more efficient and compact. The high precision and new shape make these microresonators among the best ever made with chalcogenide glass.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to use these microresonators to build real-world tools like sensitive chemical detectors and microlasers. The goal is to produce these devices in large numbers for practical applications in navigation, healthcare, and advanced computing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – CU Boulder scientists have created highly efficient tiny optical devices that trap light longer using smooth racetrack curves and special glass, opening doors to better sensors and lasers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des micro-pistes optiques ultra-efficaces qui capturent la lumière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des chercheurs de l'Université du Colorado à Boulder travaillent sur de minuscules dispositifs appelés micro-résonateurs. Ces dispositifs retiennent la lumière dans un petit espace, ce qui permet d'amplifier son intensité pour des usages comme la détection de produits chimiques ou l'amélioration des lasers.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'équipe a conçu de nouveaux micro-résonateurs en forme de piste de course, utilisant des courbes douces inspirées par la conception des routes pour éviter que la lumière ne s'échappe. Ils ont fabriqué ces dispositifs avec un verre spécial appelé chalcogénure, en utilisant des machines très précises dans un laboratoire propre. Les tests ont montré que ces dispositifs retiennent la lumière mieux que la plupart des autres fabriqués avec des matériaux similaires.

**Impact** – Ce design réduit la perte d'énergie et permet à la lumière de circuler plus longtemps à l'intérieur du dispositif, ce qui est important pour rendre les capteurs, les microlasers et la technologie quantique plus efficaces et compacts. La grande précision et la nouvelle forme font de ces micro-résonateurs parmi les meilleurs jamais réalisés avec du verre chalcogénure.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'utiliser ces micro-résonateurs pour fabriquer des outils concrets comme des détecteurs chimiques sensibles et des microlasers. L'objectif est de produire ces dispositifs en grande quantité pour des applications pratiques dans la navigation, la santé et l'informatique avancée.

**Message clé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques de l'Université du Colorado ont créé de petits dispositifs optiques très efficaces qui retiennent la lumière plus longtemps grâce à des courbes de piste de course douces et un verre spécial, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs capteurs et lasers.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan micropistas ópticas que atrapan la luz con mínima pérdida
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Colorado en Boulder trabajan en dispositivos diminutos llamados microresonadores. Estos dispositivos atrapan y mantienen la luz en un espacio pequeño, permitiendo que aumente su intensidad para usos como detectar químicos o mejorar láseres.

**Qué pasó** – El equipo creó nuevos microresonadores con forma de pista de carreras, usando curvas suaves inspiradas en el diseño de carreteras para evitar que la luz se escape. Fabricaron estos dispositivos con un vidrio especial llamado calcogenuro, usando máquinas muy precisas en un laboratorio limpio. Las pruebas mostraron que estos dispositivos retienen la luz mejor que la mayoría hechos con materiales similares.

**Impacto** – Este diseño reduce la pérdida de energía y permite que la luz circule más tiempo dentro del dispositivo, lo que es importante para hacer sensores, microláseres y tecnología cuántica más eficientes y compactos. La alta precisión y la nueva forma hacen que estos microresonadores estén entre los mejores hechos con vidrio de calcogenuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar estos microresonadores para construir herramientas reales como detectores químicos sensibles y microláseres. El objetivo es producir estos dispositivos en grandes cantidades para aplicaciones prácticas en navegación, salud y computación avanzada.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos de CU Boulder han creado dispositivos ópticos diminutos y muy eficientes que atrapan la luz por más tiempo usando curvas suaves en forma de pista y vidrio especial, abriendo camino a mejores sensores y láseres.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a smooth, glowing racetrack-shaped optical microresonator glowing softly on a sleek chip surface, with radiant light waves gently curving and circulating inside its elongated loop, surrounded by subtle abstract patterns suggesting precise nanofabrication and delicate glass materials, all rendered in soft natural hues without human figures or text.

articles.title: Loophole found that makes quantum cloning possible
articles.summary: Duplicating the information held in quantum computers was thought to be impossible thanks to the no-cloning theorem, but researchers have now found a workaround
articles.content:
Duplicating the information held in quantum computers was thought to be impossible thanks to the no-cloning theorem, but researchers have now found a workaround

Backing up information on quantum computers is tricky RUSLANAS BARANAUSKAS/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY/Alamy

In quantum mechanics, the idea that quantum information can’t be duplicated is ironclad – or at least, it was. A surprising approach to backing up qubits, the basic units of quantum computers, appears to allow a sidestepping of this fundamental law of physics.

The no-cloning theorem was first discovered by researchers in the 1980s. It says that quantum states that describe all the information about a system can’t be copied. Attempting to measure the information to copy it would simply destroy the delicate quantum properties that you want to measure. This fact has proved important for quantum technologies like encryption, leading to simple protocols that prevent information from being copied and hacked.

Achim Kempf at the University of Waterloo in Canada and his colleagues have now shown that a quantum system can, in fact, be cloned, as long as the information about it is encrypted and enclosed with a special, one-off decryption key.

“You can make a lot of copies and generate redundancy in this way, but you have to encrypt the copies, and the decryption key can only be used once,” says Kempf. “This makes it compatible with a no-cloning theorem, because it says there can only ever be at most one clear, obvious, readable, non-encrypted copy of a qubit.”

Kempf and his team arrived at this surprising conclusion after working on a seemingly unrelated problem – how a quantum Wi-Fi or radio station might work. This is something that is impossible under the traditional no-cloning theorem because multiple receivers would get the same identical quantum information.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

But when Kempf and his colleagues looked at how random fluctuations, or noise, would affect the copies of information that receivers see, they realised that their system might work. “We thought, what the hell? Why does quantum noise seem to mess with the no-cloning theorem?”

After analysing the problem more carefully, Kempf and his team realised that the noise was acting as an effective encryption mechanism, garbling the original message but in a way that could be reversed. If this was done intentionally, it could be exploited as a tool.

Once they had proved this result theoretically, the team then showed that this protocol could work on a real IBM Heron 156-qubit quantum computing processor.

Because the technique is fairly resistant to noise and errors that are ubiquitous in today’s quantum computers, Kempf and his team found they could make hundreds of encrypted clones of single qubits, by repeating the process over and over again. “In fact, we ran out of real estate on the IBM processor. It holds only 156 qubits but we estimated that we can do more than 1000 encrypted clones before the [errors] make us stop.”

This modification to the no-cloning theorem could have uses for a quantum cloud storage or computing service, says Kempf. “If you send a file to Dropbox, it will save your data at least three times in three different computers that are geographically separated, so that if one is hit by fire, the other one by a flood, there’s a fair chance the third one survives,” says Kempf. “It used to be thought you can’t do that with quantum information, because you can’t clone it. But what we showed is that you can do it.”

“It’s an interesting quantum cryptographic protocol,” says Aleks Kissinger at the University of Oxford, and could have uses in quantum communication where you need some redundancy in the information being transmitted. However, it doesn’t affect the original no-cloning theorem because Kempf and his team’s method isn’t obviously cloning, he says. “It’s not so much cloning as a kind of spreading the [quantum] state to lots of other parties, in such a way that any one of those parties could later get it back,” says Kissinger. “It’s a clever trick, but I personally wouldn’t call that cloning.”

Kempf agrees. “It’s not cloning. It’s encrypted cloning,” he says. “That’s just a refinement of the no-cloning theorem.”

Journal reference Physical Review Letters DOI: 10.1103/y4y1-1ll6
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in quantum computing by finding a workaround to the fundamental no-cloning theorem, enabling encrypted cloning of quantum information. This advance has broad implications for quantum communication, cloud storage, and computing, potentially improving data redundancy and security in quantum technologies. The story is focused, detailed, and describes an actual scientific achievement with meaningful societal and technological impact.
articles.personality_title: Researchers find a way to copy quantum information safely
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers use tiny units called qubits to store information. Normally, a rule called the no-cloning theorem says you cannot copy these qubits because measuring them would destroy their delicate state. This rule has been important for keeping quantum information safe.

**What happened** – Scientists led by Achim Kempf at the University of Waterloo discovered that quantum information can be copied if it is first encrypted and paired with a special key that can only be used once. They tested this method on a real IBM quantum computer and successfully made hundreds of encrypted copies of single qubits.

**Impact** – This finding offers a new way to back up quantum information, similar to how regular data is saved multiple times in different places. It means quantum computers could store and share data more safely and reliably, even though the original no-cloning rule still applies in a stricter sense.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely explore how this encrypted cloning can improve quantum communication and cloud computing services. They may develop practical tools to protect quantum data and make quantum technologies more useful and secure.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a clever way to copy quantum information by encrypting it, opening new possibilities for storing and sharing data in quantum computers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des chercheurs trouvent un moyen de copier les informations quantiques en toute sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques utilisent de petites unités appelées qubits pour stocker des informations. Normalement, une règle appelée théorème de non-clonage interdit de copier ces qubits, car les mesurer détruirait leur état fragile. Cette règle est importante pour protéger les informations quantiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques dirigés par Achim Kempf à l'Université de Waterloo ont découvert que l'on peut copier l'information quantique si elle est d'abord cryptée et accompagnée d'une clé spéciale utilisable une seule fois. Ils ont testé cette méthode sur un ordinateur quantique IBM et ont réussi à faire des centaines de copies cryptées de qubits uniques.

**Impact** – Cette découverte offre une nouvelle façon de sauvegarder l'information quantique, comme on sauvegarde plusieurs fois les données classiques à différents endroits. Cela signifie que les ordinateurs quantiques pourraient stocker et partager des données de manière plus sûre et fiable, même si la règle originale de non-clonage reste valide dans un sens strict.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement étudier comment ce clonage crypté peut améliorer la communication et le cloud quantiques. Ils pourraient développer des outils pratiques pour protéger les données quantiques et rendre les technologies quantiques plus utiles et sécurisées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen astucieux de copier l'information quantique en la cryptant, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour stocker et partager des données dans les ordinateurs quantiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Investigadores encuentran una forma segura de copiar información cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas usan pequeñas unidades llamadas qubits para guardar información. Normalmente, una regla llamada teorema de no clonación dice que no se pueden copiar estos qubits porque medirlos destruye su estado delicado. Esta regla es importante para proteger la información cuántica.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos liderados por Achim Kempf de la Universidad de Waterloo descubrieron que la información cuántica puede copiarse si primero se encripta y se usa una llave especial que solo puede usarse una vez. Probaron este método en una computadora cuántica real de IBM y lograron hacer cientos de copias encriptadas de qubits individuales.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo ofrece una nueva forma de respaldar la información cuántica, parecido a cómo se guarda la información normal en varios lugares. Esto significa que las computadoras cuánticas podrían almacenar y compartir datos con más seguridad y confiabilidad, aunque la regla original de no clonación sigue aplicando en sentido estricto.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán cómo este clonaje encriptado puede mejorar la comunicación cuántica y los servicios en la nube. Podrían crear herramientas prácticas para proteger los datos cuánticos y hacer que las tecnologías cuánticas sean más útiles y seguras.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos encontraron una forma inteligente de copiar información cuántica encriptándola, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para almacenar y compartir datos en computadoras cuánticas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of multiple glowing, translucent orbs connected by delicate, shimmering threads, each orb partially wrapped in a protective, intricate lattice symbolizing encryption keys, set against a softly glowing abstract quantum circuit background in gentle blues and golds.
